cited_text: Look, I think you do all this work and I think it's really difficult to overestimate the amount of garbage that's involved in going public. cited_text: Your engineering projects have made no progress since the day you weren't public. And you go back to work and you have some new constituents that you have to address and communicate with. response_text: You go back to work, you have new constituents to address, but the core business is unchanged — more money in the bank, and that's about it.

cited_text: Going public gives you access to liquidity for early shareholders, whether that's the early employees or early investors. And that's great. It gives you cash for the company. And for us, we work with biggest agricultural customers, big governments, civil governments, defense and intelligence, all of those sort of actors, they want to know you're going to be around.
response_text: It gives cash to the company and — importantly — credibility: customers like governments and large agricultural players need to know you're going to be around.

cited_text: I think that where we're seeing this is that AI is now enabling the. It's basically reducing the barrier to entry so that more people can get access to this.

cited_text: But a thing that people don't know that is actually perhaps more important is that we've had a miniaturization of satellites so that the same satellite that used to cost a billion dollars and weighed 20 tons now costs a few kilograms or a few tens or hundreds of kilograms and can do just as much stuff, if not more. It's the same as the sort of mainframe computer to desktop revolution, Computer revolution for space. And it's unlocking, just like mainframes to desktops, unlock loads of applications. This is unlocking loads of applications.
response_text: It's the mainframe-to-desktop revolution for space — and just like that transition, it's unlocking loads of new applications.

cited_text: And we figured out that when launch costs come down to about $200 to $300 a kilogram, it would be cheaper, just simply cheaper to put the data centers in space. response_text: Currently sitting just over $1,000/kg; that's down ~10× in 10 years. On current Starship trajectory, Marshall expects the threshold in two to three years.

cited_text: You can put a solar panel in a sun synchronous dawn dusk orbit where you're 247 looking at the sun. So you can have a solar panel that collects and gathers five times more energy per solar panel than on the ground and you don't have to have batteries or anything else.

cited_text: I'd say all the cool stuff that we're doing with LLMs now is really based on just the text of the Internet being absorbed into these models, which is incredibly powerful already.
